from source import sieve

def test_sieve_returns_only_primes_up_to_7():
    result = sieve(7)
    expected = [2, 3, 5, 7]
    assert result == expected, (
        f"sieve(7) returned {result}, but expected {expected}. "
        "The bug uses `any(n % p > 0)` which incorrectly includes composite numbers."
    )

analysis.bug_hypothesis: The buggy code uses `any` instead of `all` in the primality test, which marks composite numbers as prime if they have at least one non-divisor among the current primes.
analysis.bug_location: sieve function, line 4: `if any(n % p > 0 for p in primes):`
analysis.trigger_condition: Any composite number n that has at least one prime divisor p where n % p > 0 (i.e., p does not divide n).
analysis.expected_vs_actual: Expected: sieve(7) returns [2, 3, 5, 7]. Actual: sieve(7) returns [2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7] because 4 is divisible by 2 (4 % 2 == 0) but `any` checks if any remainder > 0; since 4 % 3 > 0, it returns True and 4 is added.
analysis.suggested_test_strategy: Write a pytest test that asserts `sieve(7) == [2, 3, 5, 7]`. The buggy version will include composite numbers like 4 and 6, causing failure. The fixed version (using `all`) will pass.
